column1: Dream, Sleep, and Darkness Practices Across Indian Traditions

column4: Source Text
column5: Description

column4: Karling Zhi-khro and Six Yogas of Naropa (1050 CE)
column5: Dream yoga practice involving recognition of dreams as dreams (lucid dreaming) and transformation of dream content. Formalized by Padmasambhava.

column4: Guhyasamaja Tantra (8th century) and Six Yogas of Naropa (1050 CE)
column5: "Illusory body" yoga connected to dream practice, developing awareness that both waking and dreaming states are equally illusory.

column4: Trekchö Nang-jang (Dzogchen text) and Zhang Zhung Nyan Gyud (Bön text, pre-8th century)
column5: "Sleep yoga" or "yoga of clear light" practiced during the deep sleep state to maintain awareness during dreamless sleep.

column4: Vima Nyingthig (Heart Essence of Vimalamitra)
column5: "Black Quintessence" dark retreat practice where practitioners remain in complete darkness for extended periods to induce visions and realize the nature of mind.

column4: Nyingthig Yabzhi (Four Branches of Heart Essence)
column5: Practice of "leaping over" involving darkness retreats to perceive spontaneous visual phenomena (thiglés) manifesting from primordial awareness.

column4: Svacchanda Tantra and Vijñāna Bhairava Tantra (7th-8th century)
column5: Dream observation techniques found in tantric texts for receiving initiations and teachings through dreams.

column4: References in Upanishads (800-500 BCE), formalized in modern form by Swami Satyananda Saraswati
column5: "Yogic sleep," a state between waking and sleeping used for deep relaxation and to access subconscious and unconscious dimensions.

column4: Tattvartha Sutra of Umasvati and Jñānārṇava (11th century)
column5: Meditative practices done in darkness (caves or darkened rooms) to eliminate visual distractions and induce inner visions.

column4: Vigyan Bhairav Tantra and Shiva Sutras (9th century)
column5: Void meditation sometimes practiced in darkness to experience the formless nature of consciousness.

column4: Tantraloka of Abhinavagupta and Shiva Sutras
column5: Dream yoga practice integrating dream experiences with waking awareness.

column4: Vijnana Bhairava Tantra (verse 38) and Rudrayamala Tantra
column5: "Yoga of darkness" involving practices performed in darkness to cultivate inner light (jyoti).

column4: Vijñāna Bhairava Tantra (verses 59-60)
column5: Concentration on the "space of consciousness," often practiced with eyes closed in darkness.

column4: Dhyanashataka and references in Acaranga Sutra
column5: Cave-based meditations where darkness-induced visions were interpreted as spiritual insights.

column4: Rudrayamala Tantra and Kaulajnananirnaya
column5: "Night wakefulness" practices where practitioners remained alert throughout the night to access liminal states of consciousness.

column4: Hathayoga Pradipika (chapter 4, verses 49-53)
column5: Dream yoga techniques mentioned in this medieval hatha yoga text.

column4: Siddha Siddhanta Paddhati and Gorakshashataka
column5: "Invisible absorption" meditation sometimes practiced in darkness to perceive subtle energy bodies.

column4: Mālinīvijayottara Tantra and Kularnava Tantra
column5: "Darkness state meditation" using darkness to induce visionary experiences and enhance inner perception.

column4: Khecarividya and later in Hathayoga Pradipika (1450 CE)
column5: Advanced tongue lock practice sometimes done in darkness to induce visionary states and perception of inner light.

column4: Bardo Thödol (formally compiled 14th century) and Six Yogas of Naropa
column5: Intermediate state practices, including dream-like states between life and death, formalized in the Bardo Thödol (Tibetan Book of the Dead).

column4: Vijñāna Bhairava Tantra (verse 37) and Kularnava Tantra
column5: "Darkness meditation" practices to cultivate inner illumination through contrast with external darkness.

column4: Bogar 7000 and Tirumantiram of Tirumular
column5: Rejuvenation practices sometimes performed in darkness to facilitate visionary experiences and bodily transformation.

column4: Six Yogas of Naropa and Chakrasamvara Tantra commentaries
column5: Inner heat yoga combined with darkness practices to induce luminous visions and altered states.

column4: Gheraṇḍa Saṃhitā (verse 2.29) and Advaya Taraka Upanishad
column5: "Star yoga" focusing on perception of inner lights and colors, often practiced with eyes closed or in darkness.

column4: Six Yogas of Naropa and Chakrasamvara Tantra commentaries
column5: Consciousness transfer practice sometimes combined with dream yoga for preparation for death.

column4: Netra Tantra and Svacchanda Tantra
column5: "Divine eye" practices involving darkness meditation to develop subtle perception.
